conversation.message: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.

You've been smoking bidis for 45 years, and that's a long time to develop a habit. What was it like for you when you first started smoking bidis, and was there anything in particular that made you pick up the habit?
It started when I was a teenager. All my friends smoked, so I did too. It was just something to do, you know?
It sounds like your friends played a big role in you starting to smoke bidis back then. What's it like for you now, when you're with friends or in social situations where people are smoking?
It's... normal, I guess. I don't think about it much. I just do it like everyone else.
You don't think about it much when you're with friends, that's helpful to know. How do you feel about quitting or reducing your bidis use when you see your friends smoking?
It's hard to think about quitting when they're all doing it. It feels weird, I don't know, like I'd be left out or something.
You're worried about feeling left out when you see your friends smoking. How do you think you'd feel if you suggested to your friends that they join you in quitting or reducing their bidis use?
No, I don't think they'd be interested. They're used to smoking. I'm the one who's trying to stop.

You're feeling like you're the only one trying to quit, and that might make it harder for you. How do you think you'd feel about finding a new group of friends or activities that don't involve smoking?
That's alright, I guess. But it's hard to find new people who'd be interested in the same things as me. I live in a small place, people know each other for years.
You feel like you'd struggle to find new people with similar interests in your small community. Do you think there's anything you've always wanted to try or explore, but haven't had the chance yet?
I've always wanted to learn more about gardening, like a real expert. I just do it for a living, but I mean, I'd like to know more about it.
You've shared that you've always wanted to learn more about gardening. What is it about being an expert in gardening that really appeals to you?
I think it's just that I'd be able to make our farm better. My family's been doing this for years, but I want to make sure it's running smoothly for my kids, you know?

I understand that making your farm better for your kids is a big motivator for you. How do you think giving up cigarettes would impact your ability to spend quality time with your kids and be a role model for them?
It would be good for them, I guess. They see me struggling to breathe sometimes, that can't be good.

You're thinking about how your kids see you struggling with your breathing, that's really tough. What's the hardest part about quitting cigarettes for you - is it the physical addiction, the social aspect, or something else entirely?
The morning, it's the hardest time. I wake up craving a bidi. I know it's bad, but I just can't help it.
You're really struggling with morning cravings, that's a tough one. Can you imagine what you might be doing differently in those first few hours of the morning that could help reduce your cravings?
I guess I could try drinking some tea or something. It's what my wife does in the mornings.

I love that you're thinking of your wife's morning routine as a potential inspiration. Are you open to trying a gentle daily routine like your wife's, and would you be willing to start with just one morning activity to see how it goes?
Yes, that sounds alright. Just having a cup of tea. I can try that.
You're excited to try having a cup of tea in the morning, that's a great start. Will you commit to doing this same morning routine for the next 3-4 days, just to see if it helps reduce your cravings?
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
You've been smoking bidis for about 8 years now, and it's become a daily habit for you. What do you think triggers you to smoke, like when you're feeling stressed, bored, or in certain situations?
It's when my coworkers smoke on site and offer me one. They say "have one, yaar" and it's hard to say no.

You feel like you're struggling to resist the offer of a bidi when your coworkers invite you to join in, and it sounds like it's become a hard habit to break. What do you think would be the most difficult part for you to give up about smoking bidis?
My wife will be angry, that's a problem. We're already short on money and I'll have to find another way to manage our expenses.

It sounds like you're worried about your wife's reaction and also concerned about the financial impact of quitting smoking bidis on your household. Can you tell me, what's been your wife's attitude towards your smoking in the past, and how do you think she'll feel if you quit?
She's always told me it's not good for me but she's not said much. I think she'll be happy, but she's already stressed about the money we're struggling with, and this won't make it any easier.

You're worried about your wife's reaction and the added stress on your household finances. 

How would you feel about developing a plan with me to gradually reduce the number of bidis you smoke, so that by the time you quit, it's not a drastic change?
I don't know...it sounds like a lot of work. I've tried quitting before, but I couldn't do it. I'm not sure I can do it again. What's the point of trying if it's not going to work?

You feel like quitting is a lot of work and that you've tried before without success, which makes you wonder what the point of trying again is. Can you think of anything that might make this attempt different from the others?
My wife's always telling me to stop, but this doctor at the hospital said I need to go to a programme like this, so maybe it's not just me trying to quit this time.

You're saying this doctor's advice might give you a sense of accountability and motivation that's different from just your own decisions to quit. Can you imagine what specific steps you could take to make the most out of this experience and the doctor's guidance?

I suppose I could write down how many bidis I smoke each day, and try to cut that down gradually. But it's not like I can just stop buying bidis one day and be done with it. What happens when my coworkers offer me one?

You're worried about your coworkers offering you bidis, which can make it hard to stick to your plan. Would it help if we came up with a simple plan for how you can politely decline their offers and redirect the conversation?
That might help a bit. But what if they keep offering me bidis every day? Can I really do this for the rest of my life?

You're feeling uncertain about your ability to maintain this change over the long term. Can you think of a time when you successfully overcame a challenging situation that you didn't think you could handle?

There was this one time when our power was cut off during the winter, and my wife and I had to figure out how to cook dinner. We managed to find a way to use a candle and a kettle, and we were okay. But that was just for one night. Quitting smoking is a different thing altogether.

You're drawing on a past experience where you and your wife were able to overcome a difficult situation together. Can you imagine how it would feel to have your wife's support in staying smoke-free, and how that might impact your chances of quitting?
It might make it a bit easier. Maybe having her support would help me stay on track, but what if I fail? She's already stressed about our money situation...

You're worried about failing and feeling like it would add to your wife's stress. Would it help to imagine how she would feel if you were to succeed in quitting smoking, and how that could bring a sense of relief and support to your relationship?
I suppose it's possible. But it's not like I've ever seen anyone else quit for good. I don't know if it's even possible for me.

You're feeling uncertain about whether quitting smoking is even possible for you. Would you be open to trying something that might help you visualize a life without bidis, like imagining yourself enjoying a smoke-free morning routine?
I guess it couldn't hurt to try. But what if I just end up feeling worse and can't do it?

You're concerned about potentially feeling worse if you try to quit, but let's explore that idea: what would be the biggest difference in your life if you did manage to reduce your bidi smoking, even if it's just a small step?
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.
You've been smoking bidis and eating paan for about 7-8 years now. What was going on in your life around the time you started smoking?
Started after my father-in-law died. I was 25, still a young man. Needed something to calm my nerves, I think.

You started smoking as a way to calm your nerves after your father-in-law passed away. That must have been a really tough time for you. What emotions do you still experience when you're reminded of him or think about that difficult period in your life?
Still miss him, my father-in-law. His death was a big change. Hard to explain... it's been so long, but it's still a void.
It sounds like his death left a big void in your life, and that's still felt today. What are some specific situations or moments that remind you of him now?

Well, when I sit at home, it's hard not to think about him. I think of all the times he used to visit and we'd talk for hours. But, since I'm retired, it's just me at home now. Most of the time, I'm alone, trying to sleep.

You've been describing how aloneness triggers your nostalgia for your father-in-law and makes you think about smoking to cope with the void. Have you ever tried calling a friend or family member to talk while you're feeling lonely at home?
My daughters live far away, and my son doesn't live with us. I have friends, but they're all busy. We don't talk often. I'm used to being alone, I suppose.

It sounds like you're used to being alone, and that's a big part of your life now. Would you be open to calling a friend or family member to talk with you for a little while when you're feeling lonely at home?

It's hard to pick up the phone. I get anxious thinking about what to say. And I don't want to bother them when they're busy. Maybe my daughters could help me... they're always saying I need to stay social.

You're feeling anxious about calling your daughters, and that's making it hard for you to reach out to them for support. Would you be willing to make a simple phone call to one of them and ask for their support in staying connected, even if it's just a conversation starter like "Hey, I'm feeling lonely, can we catch up for a bit?"
Maybe. But what if they're busy? Or they can't talk right away? It's hard to explain... it's been a few years since my wife passed. I'm still getting used to not having her here.

It sounds like the thought of not having your wife around is still a big challenge for you. How do you think reaching out to your daughters for support could help you stay connected to her memory while also reducing your reliance on smoking to cope with loneliness?

Reaching out to them might help me feel less alone. I still talk to my wife's pictures, but it's not the same. If I can talk to my daughters, it might feel like... she's still with me in a way. Smoking just numbs the feeling, but I don't think it's really helping.

It sounds like talking to your daughters could be a way to feel more connected to your wife's memory, which might help you with your loneliness. Would you be willing to make a phone call to your daughters and see how it goes, even if it's just for a short conversation?
Okay... I'll try it today. But if they're busy, I don't want to feel like I'm bothering them.
It takes a lot of courage to reach out, and I'm so proud of you for taking this step – wishing you peace and connection as you move forward.
Thank you... I just want to feel better.

Given that we're in the Planning phase, I need to suggest a technique to help you overcome your addiction. Now that you've acknowledged reaching out to your daughters might help, how do you think you could use this connection to replace the habit of smoking when you feel lonely?

I think I could... call them when I feel the urge to smoke. Talk to them, or even just think about the conversations we'll have later. Maybe it'll distract me from wanting a bidi or paan. And then I can also work on reducing my paan habit, like I said I was trying to do already.

You've already thought of using your daughters as a distraction when you feel the urge to smoke, and that's a great idea. Will you make a mental note to call them as soon as you feel the urge coming on, so you can use that connection to help you stay on track?
I'll try. But what about when I wake up at 3 AM and can't sleep? How do I stay away from a bidi then?
You've got a clear plan in place for challenging situations, and I have faith that you'll stay strong when you need it most.
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
How long have you been smoking?
Around 12 years. Started when I was a young teacher, trying to calm down after yelling at my students.
You started smoking to calm down after stressful situations, like yelling at your students. Can you think of a different way to calm down now, besides reaching for a cigarette?

Walking outside, I guess. Or just taking a few deep breaths. But it's not the same, you know? It's like, my body's already in a certain state and a cigarette somehow fixes it, even if it's just for a short time.

You feel like walking outside or taking deep breaths helps, but it's not the same as having a cigarette. Can you imagine incorporating a small, portable object that reminds you to breathe deeply whenever you feel stressed, like a stress ball or a small stone?
I don't know...I mean, it sounds like a good idea, but I'm not sure I'd use it. I guess it's not as convenient as just having a cigarette with me.

You're concerned about the convenience of using a small object to remind you to breathe deeply. Would carrying a small object like that be more appealing if it was something you could easily keep in your pocket or purse?
I suppose that's better. But would it really work, though? I mean, cigarettes work for me, you know? They calm me down.
You're still unsure if a small object would help you calm down. What if this object became a habit, like checking your phone when you feel stressed, and over time, it replaced the urge to smoke?
That sounds plausible, I guess. But it's hard to imagine actually doing it. I mean, I've been smoking for so long...it feels like a part of how I deal with things.
You've been using cigarettes as a way to cope with stress and emotions for a long time. Can you imagine what you'd need to feel confident that this small object would help you manage those moments?

I don't know...maybe if I saw someone else using it and it actually worked for them? Or if it was something that really resonated with me, like a specific stone or something. I'd need to feel like it's worth trying, I guess.

You mentioned seeing someone else using it and it actually working for them would help you feel more confident in trying this small object. Have you considered reaching out to that person who quit smoking and successfully uses this object to see if they'd be willing to share their experience with you?
You know my son's therapist? She was actually the one who suggested this small object thing. Maybe I could talk to her about it, then.

Given that we're in the planning phase and I need to suggest a technique, I'll focus on building on your idea of talking to your son's therapist. 

You mentioned your son's therapist suggested the small object, and it might be helpful to ask her how she used it to manage cravings and if she'd be willing to share her experience with you.
Yeah, that's a good idea. I could ask her about it. Maybe she could give me some tips or share how it helped her. I'm still not sure, though...I mean, this is my way of dealing with stress and anger.
Based on your idea of talking to your son's therapist, what specific questions would you like to ask her about how she used this small object to manage cravings and stress?
Like, what kind of object did she use? And how often did she use it when she felt stressed? And was it really effective?

Now that you have a clear idea of what questions you'd like to ask your son's therapist, would it be helpful to actually reach out to her and schedule a conversation about this before our next session?
Yeah, that's a good idea. It'll show me that this method might actually work, and it'll help me feel more prepared for our next session here.
Now that you've decided to talk to your son's therapist, how would you feel using a similar object as a personal reminder to manage your cravings and stress outside of our sessions?
I think I could try it. It's not like I'm committing to quitting or anything, just giving it a shot. And if it doesn't work, I can always come back here and we can try something else.
You're open to giving it a shot. What kind of small object do you think might be helpful for you to use as a reminder to manage cravings and stress?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge you're considering quitting, even if it doesn't feel like a priority right now. What's the first thing that comes to mind when you think about quitting smoking?
Customers will get annoyed with me. They're always barging in, wanting things fast, and I get a bit stressed. I use cigarettes to calm down.

You're worried that without cigarettes, you won't be able to handle the stress of dealing with demanding customers. How do you think it would feel if you had a different way to calm down in those situations?

I guess it would be easier, but I don't know what else to do. I've never really thought about it before. Taking deep breaths or something, maybe? But it's hard to remember when someone's yelling at you.

You've tried taking deep breaths before, but it's hard to remember to do that when you're in the middle of a stressful situation. Would you be open to keeping a stress journal to track when you feel like smoking and what you were feeling at those moments?
I don't know...I don't want to be writing all the time, and what's the point of it, anyway? It's not like it's going to change how customers are.
You're not sold on the idea of a stress journal, and that's okay. Can you think of one situation where you've managed to resist smoking, even when feeling stressed?

My wife was at her sister's place a few days ago, and I was home alone. I was feeling really annoyed, but I didn't smoke. I remember I just went out to the backyard and paced around instead, and then I started listening to music. It wasn't great, but it worked for a bit.

You managed to resist smoking when you were feeling stressed and your wife was away, and you used pacing and listening to music to calm down. Would you be willing to try using music as a stress-reduction technique more intentionally, like you did that time, but this time, right when you feel the urge to smoke?
I suppose it couldn't hurt to try listening to music more often. But I'm not making any promises – I still need to find my way around the shop when customers are being difficult.
Now that you've agreed to try listening to music more often, how do you think you can make it a consistent habit, especially in moments when you feel stressed and want to smoke?

I can put one of my favorite songs on my phone, so it's always there. I can even leave it on the counter or something, so when I'm in a rush, I know what to do. But what if I'm out on the road, you know, when I usually smoke during tea time?

You've got a good solution for when you're at home, but now you need to think about how to apply that to situations like when you're driving during tea time. How might you be able to listen to music or a similar stress-reduction technique while you're on the road?
I guess I could put some music on my phone, same as at home. Or maybe I can even use one of those podcasts I've been meaning to listen to...I just never had the time.

You've had some good ideas about how to use music and podcasts while you're driving, but now I'd like to explore how you can make time for these stress-reduction techniques when you're feeling rushed or have a lot on your plate.
I don't know...my day's always packed. I've got to count the money, order supplies, deal with customers...how am I supposed to find time to sit around and listen to music?

You feel overwhelmed by the demands of the day, and listening to music seems like a luxury you can't afford. Can you tell me more about what makes you feel like you need to rush through your day, and is there anything that could help you prioritize your well-being amidst all the responsibilities?

It's just the shop, you know? It's my livelihood. I feel like if I'm not constantly on top of things, someone's going to take advantage of me. And my wife's always talking about how I'm burning out, but what choice do I have?

It sounds like the shop's demands are taking a toll on you, and you feel like you have no choice but to keep up the pace. Can you imagine what would happen if you actually took a break from the shop for just a few minutes each day, and what might be the benefits of doing so?

I guess it couldn't hurt to take a few minutes, but it's hard to picture myself just stepping out or closing up the shop early. Maybe if I talked to my wife about it, though...she's always saying things like that, so maybe she'd be on board with it.

You're starting to consider taking a break from the shop, that's a great start. Would it be helpful to set aside a specific time each day, like right after opening the shop, to take a short break and do something relaxing, like listening to music?
Yeah, maybe. Like right after opening, that's a good idea. I could just step back, take a few deep breaths, and then get started. That way, I'm not feeling so frazzled from the get-go.
That sounds like a plan to take a few deep breaths right after opening the shop. Would you be willing to make a small commitment to do that every day, even if it's just for a minute or two?
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

It sounds like you've been feeling really discouraged about quitting, and it's understandable that you're not sure why you're here. What's been going through your mind when you've tried to quit in the past, and what's kept you from being successful?

It's the anxiety, I guess. And the weight gain. I just couldn't handle it. I'd get anxious, I'd light up again. And the weight, I thought I'd be heavier than I already am. That's what stopped me from quitting for good.

It sounds like you've been using smoking as a temporary way to cope with anxiety and weight gain. Would you be open to trying something called "urge surfing" - a technique that might help you ride out those anxious moments without reaching for a cigarette?
I don't know...I've tried all sorts of things, and nothing's worked. What's this "urge surfing" thing? It sounds like some new-fangled program. How do you do it?

You're feeling a bit skeptical about trying something new, and that's understandable given your past experiences. Can you tell me what makes you hesitant about trying a different approach, like "urge surfing"?

I just don't know if it'll work. I've tried so many things, and I've always ended up back where I started. I'm worried it's just going to be another thing that doesn't help. And what if it makes me even more anxious? I'm already anxious, I don't want to be more anxious.
You're worried that "urge surfing" might not work and might even make you more anxious. What's the one thing that would need to happen for you to feel comfortable giving it a try?

I guess if you could show me it works for someone else, or if you could explain it in a way that makes sense to me, then I might be more willing to try it. But I need to know it's not just some magic trick that's going to fail me like everything else has.

Let me see if I can explain "urge surfing" in a way that makes sense to you. Can you imagine riding out a wave at the beach - it comes in, it peaks, and then it goes back out - would you be willing to try to ride out your urge to smoke like that?
I think I get it, kind of...but how do I know when the "wave" of my urge is going to subside? And what if it just keeps coming back, like the waves keep coming back in?

You're worried about knowing when the urge will subside and whether it will keep coming back. Can you think of a situation where you've successfully managed an uncomfortable feeling or situation in the past, that might give you some confidence to apply it to your urges to smoke?

Well...like when I had to give a presentation at work and I was really nervous, but I just took a few deep breaths and got through it. Maybe it's like that. Maybe I can ride out the urge to smoke for a little while, and it'll pass. But what if it's not just about riding it out, like with the presentation, but it's really about not being able to stop myself from smoking?

You're thinking that maybe you can apply the same calm approach to riding out your urge to smoke, like you did with the presentation. Can you imagine what would happen if you were able to stay calm and ride out one urge to smoke without giving in?

..if I could just do it once, I think it might give me the confidence to try it again. And again. Maybe it would start to feel more normal not to smoke, you know? Like, I don't know, maybe I could start to feel like I can handle the urges, instead of feeling so out of control.

Now that you're thinking about the confidence boost that can come from riding out one urge to smoke, how would you like to start practicing this "urge surfing" technique in a low-stakes situation, like when you're not feeling as anxious or stressed?

Maybe when I'm just sitting at home, watching TV or something. I could try riding out the urge to smoke while I'm just sitting there. Like, if I feel like I want a cigarette, I'll just stay in the chair and wait it out. See if I can do it. Does that sound okay?

You're thinking of starting with a low-stakes situation, like sitting at home watching TV, to practice riding out the urge to smoke. Would you be willing to also set a time limit for yourself to try this technique, like 10 minutes, to see if you can stay committed to waiting out the urge?

Yeah...I guess that would be okay. 10 minutes sounds doable, I think. If I can make it through 10 minutes without smoking, then that's something. And if I can't, then I'll just try again tomorrow, or the next day, or...I don't know.

You're feeling more open to trying this "urge surfing" technique, and you've decided to start with a low-stakes situation. Are you willing to track your progress, maybe keeping a journal or log, to see how often you're able to wait out an urge to smoke for 10 minutes?

I don't know...I don't want to feel like I'm failing or something. But I guess it might be helpful to see if I'm actually making progress. Yeah, okay. I'll try it. But you have to promise me I won't be judged if I don't do it perfectly.
You're taking a courageous step towards exploring this new approach, and I'm here to support you, not to judge your progress.
I think I can do this. I'll start tracking it and see how it goes. Thanks for listening.
Now that you're feeling more confident about trying "urge surfing" and tracking your progress, how will you remind yourself to stick with this new approach when the going gets tough?
Look, I work at a dhaba and everyone smokes here. It's part of the culture. I don't really want to quit, but the doctor insisted I come.
It sounds like working at the dhaba makes it really hard for you to avoid smoking. How long have you been smoking?
12 years now. I started when I was 22, when I got this job. It's just something you do with all the guys, you know?
It sounds like smoking has become a big part of your social routine with the guys at the dhaba. What's the atmosphere usually like when you're working and everyone around you is smoking?
It's normal, you know? Everyone's smoking, it's like the whole atmosphere changes. You get a break, we all sit down, take a smoke, talk about stuff. It's just our thing.
It sounds like the break times with your coworkers are a big part of the social aspect of smoking for you. What do you usually talk about during those breaks?
Work stuff, sports, movies... just stuff like that. Nothing serious. It's good for us to take a break, catch up.
It sounds like those breaks are a chance for you to unwind and socialize with your coworkers. What happens if you can't take a break and smoke during a particularly stressful shift?
Then I just try to get through it, you know? It's not easy, but I manage. Usually after the rush dies down, I take a few minutes and catch up on a smoke. It helps me calm down.

It sounds like smoking has been a way for you to calm down during stressful shifts, and it helps you get through those tough times. What do you think would happen if you didn't have smoking to rely on next time you're feeling stressed?
I don't know... I'd probably just find another way to calm down, I guess. Maybe I'd talk to someone, or just take a walk outside. But it's hard to imagine not smoking at all, you know?
It sounds like you're considering other ways to calm down when you're feeling stressed, and that's a great start. What's holding you back from making a decision to quit smoking right now?
I don't know if I'm ready to give up smoking completely. It's not just about stopping, it's about... being part of the gang, you know? If I quit, I feel like I'll be different from everyone else.

I understand that you're worried about feeling left out if you quit smoking. Can you imagine a way to be part of the gang without smoking, like joining in on conversations or activities that don't involve cigarettes?

I guess so... I mean, we play cricket together, watch movies, eat food together... there's a lot we do that doesn't involve smoking. But it's hard to see myself doing those things without having a smoke, you know?

Based on your concerns about feeling left out if you quit smoking, I'd like to try something that might help you see yourself as part of the group in a new way. Would you be willing to imagine yourself quitting smoking for just one week, and then see how that feels before making a longer commitment?
I don't know... One week sounds a little too long, I think. Can't it be shorter? Like three days, or something?
Let's try a shorter timeframe. Would you feel more comfortable with a two-day trial, just to see how it goes?
Yeah, I can do two days. But if it's too hard, I'm not gonna make it up to a week, okay?
Now that we've decided on a two-day trial, what are some specific strategies you can use to cope with stress during those two days, since you mentioned stress is a big trigger for you to smoke?

Like I said, I guess I could take a walk outside, talk to someone... maybe try to stay focused on work instead of getting so tense. And if I'm feeling really stuck, I can try to find a different way to take a break, like eating something or reading a book.

That sounds like you have a good starting point with those ideas. Are you open to the idea of keeping a small journal to track your stress levels and what coping strategies work best for you during those two days?
I don't know... Writing in a journal sounds like more work, you know? But if it'll help me figure out what works, sure. I'll try it out.
It was a pleasure working with you today, and I wish you all the best as you take this next step.
I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.
It sounds like that really tough week at work was a trigger for you to start smoking again. What makes you think you can quit this time, and what would it mean for you to be smoke-free?

I've been successful in quitting before, I know I can do it again. Being smoke-free would mean I'm in control of my life, not cigarettes. It would be nice to feel like I'm healthier, you know, for my family and myself.

You've had success quitting before and you're confident you can do it again. It's interesting that you mention being in control of your life - how do you think you can use that sense of control to handle stressful days at work, like the one that triggered you to start smoking again?

I guess I could think of some strategies beforehand, like taking a walk or meditating during breaks. But it's hard when you're in the middle of a crisis. You're not really thinking about what you should be doing, you're just reacting.

It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed and reacting impulsively during a crisis. What if you created a personal crisis plan that outlines some specific coping strategies you can quickly refer to when you're in a tough situation?
That's not a bad idea. I mean, I've heard of people doing stress plans before. But I'm not sure what I would put in it. I don't want to just list a bunch of things that won't work.
What would be one thing you've tried in the past that you think could be adapted or combined with other strategies to help you cope with stress at work?

Taking a break and doing some exercise was helpful when I quit last time. I could maybe do that again, or find other ways to get moving when I'm feeling stressed. But it's hard when you're in a meeting or at your desk all day.

You've had success with taking a break and exercising last time, what adaptations can you make to incorporate small movements into your daily routine, like stretching or quick walks, even when you're stuck at your desk or in a meeting?

You're really taking ownership of your smoking and drinking habits, and it's great that your wife is supporting you in making a change. How do you think you could use a plan to manage your morning cravings, which seem to be a consistent trigger for you?
I guess I do most of my smoking in the morning, after waking up and after breakfast. If I'm getting ready for work, I feel I need something to calm me down.

Given your morning cravings seem to be a key trigger, I'm wondering if you'd be open to trying a mindfulness exercise, like deep breathing, to calm yourself down instead of reaching for a cigarette when you're getting ready for work?

I suppose I can try it, for one morning, just to see. But what if it doesn't work? I don't want to feel like a failure.
Given your concerns about feeling like a failure, how do you think we could reframe this one-time experiment as a success, even if deep breathing doesn't completely eliminate your craving?
I mean, it's just one morning, right? Even if it doesn't work out, I'm not giving up or anything. And if it does work, that's still a good thing.
Let's build on your positive attitude about giving deep breathing a try. Are you willing to commit to practicing deep breathing every morning for the next week to see how it affects your cravings?
A week sounds doable, I guess. But what if it starts to feel like too much? I don't know if I can keep it up for that long.
You're worried about feeling overwhelmed. What if we made deep breathing a simple habit, like brushing your teeth in the morning, so it becomes a part of your daily routine?
That's a good idea. Maybe if it's just one of the things I do automatically every morning, I won't even think about it anymore.

Now that we're thinking of making deep breathing a habit, let's talk about how you can make it more convenient to do in the mornings - do you think you could find a quiet spot in your home where you can easily go to practice deep breathing without being distracted?
There's a small room off the kitchen that I never really use for anything. It's quiet and it's not on the way to anything, so it might work.
Now that you've found a quiet spot, what's the first thing you'll do tomorrow morning to create a habit of going there to practice deep breathing?
I'll leave a cup there for myself in the morning, so it's a reminder to go sit down and practice.
It sounds like you've found a thoughtful way to establish a consistent routine for taking care of yourself – I wish you continued success with this practice.
I'll try my best. Thanks for helping me come up with a plan.
It was a pleasure supporting you in this process – may you feel more grounded and at peace moving forward.
I feel a bit more hopeful, I guess. I'll try not to think too much about it, just stick to the plan.
You're taking a brave and intentional step forward – sending you love and support.
I've been smoking for about 2 years. My husband and I are planning to have a baby soon. I know I need to stop.
You're motivated to quit for your future child's health, which is a wonderful reason. What specific situations or moments make you reach for a cigarette right now?
It's usually at work breaks - when I'm stressed or just need a minute to myself. Sometimes after dinner, too. One cigarette helps me unwind, I guess.

Those work breaks and after-dinner moments can be challenging. Would you be open to trying a "5-minute pause" when you feel the urge to smoke, where you take a few deep breaths and do something else instead of reaching for a cigarette?

I've heard of that before, but not really thought about it too much. How would that exactly work? I get anxious about quitting, so if I'm just sitting there feeling impatient, it might be hard to wait 5 minutes.

You're unsure about the 5-minute pause technique because it might be hard to wait when you're feeling impatient. Can you think of any small activities that might help you distract yourself and make the wait more manageable?
Walking around the block or doing some stretching exercises might help. And maybe drinking water or eating a healthy snack. I don't want to just sit there, that would be too hard.

It sounds like you're thinking of ways to distract yourself when you feel the urge to smoke. Can you imagine incorporating some of those activities into your daily routine, like taking a short walk during work breaks, to see if it could help make the transition to quitting feel more manageable?
That's not a bad idea. But what about when I'm really stressed about work or stuff at home? Those are the times when I usually smoke. I'm not sure if a short walk will be enough to deal with that.

You're still unsure about how to handle those really tough moments when you're stressed about work or stuff at home. Would you be open to trying a "grounding technique" where you focus on your five senses to ground yourself in the present moment, rather than getting caught up in worries about work or home?

I don't know... I'm not really sure how that would work. I guess it sounds a bit too new-agey for me. Can you explain it some more? Maybe give me an example of how it would help in a situation where I'm really stressed?

Let me see if I can help clarify the grounding technique in a way that feels more practical and approachable to you. Have you ever found yourself getting lost in thought while doing something else, like when you're driving and before you know it, you've arrived at your destination, and you can't even remember the last few minutes?
Yeah, that's happened to me. But I don't know how that's going to help me with quitting smoking.

You're still wondering how the grounding technique might help with quitting smoking. Can you imagine being able to bring your attention back to the present moment, to your breath or your surroundings, when you feel the urge to smoke?

I guess that might be helpful. But it's hard for me to imagine actually doing it when I'm in the middle of a stressful moment. Maybe if you had some kind of plan or schedule for how to quit, that might make it feel more manageable.

You're thinking a plan or schedule might make quitting feel more manageable. Would you be open to creating a simple daily routine for the next few days that includes activities and reminders to help you stay on track and build confidence in your ability to handle stressful moments without smoking?

That sounds like a good idea. But I don't know... what kind of things would I have to do? And what's the timeline looking like? Are we talking about quitting for a week, a month? I need to know how long this is going to take.

Let's focus on creating a manageable quit plan together. What specific activities or self-care practices would you like to include in your daily routine to help you cope with stress and cravings when you quit smoking?

I'd like to keep doing my yoga practice, and maybe add some meditation at home. I've also been wanting to try journaling, it seems like it might help me process my thoughts and feelings better. And, like we talked about before, going for short walks during breaks might be helpful too.
Let's make these activities a part of a daily routine to help you stay on track. How can we incorporate these self-care practices into your daily schedule, so it becomes a habit?

Let's say I quit smoking by the end of the month, and we can make a schedule for that. I have school commitments, so I'll need to make sure I'm not overwhelmed with extra activities. Can we start with, like, small goals each week and then build up from there?

Let's start breaking down your month into smaller chunks to make quitting feel more achievable. How many weeks do you think you'll need to prepare for quitting by the end of the month, considering your school commitments and other responsibilities?

I'd say I need at least 2-3 weeks to get into a routine, and then 1-2 weeks to actually quit. That way, I can get used to doing yoga and meditation and journaling without feeling overwhelmed by the time the quit date rolls around.

Now that we have a rough outline of your quit plan, let's think about how we can use your journaling practice to track your progress and identify patterns with your triggers. Can you imagine writing down specific situations where you usually feel the urge to smoke and how you cope with them during those moments?
I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

That's a significant increase in smoking, and I can imagine how isolating working from home must be. What do you think is different about your work-from-home environment that might be contributing to your increased smoking?

I think it's because I get easily distracted and context-switch between tasks a lot. I'll be working on a project and then get sidetracked by something else, so I take a break and end up smoking a cigarette. It's just what I do to get my mind cleared. And sometimes I feel like I'm stuck at home, so I smoke to get out of this feeling of cabin fever, I guess.

It sounds like you're using smoking as a way to cope with feelings of distraction and cabin fever while working from home. Have you considered setting up a separate workspace, even just a different room, to help you stay focused on tasks and avoid the triggers that lead you to smoke?

I've thought about that, but I don't know... it's not that easy to rearrange the whole house, and I'm not sure it would really solve the problem. Plus, even if I do move to a different room, I'll still be stuck here alone, so the issue doesn't really get addressed, right?

It sounds like rearranging your workspace is a good idea, but you're worried it won't fully address the underlying issue of feeling stuck and alone at home. Are you open to exploring other ways to manage feelings of isolation while working from home?

I guess so. I just don't know what else to do, you know? I've tried working out at home, but it's not the same as going for a walk outside, I think. And I don't really feel like I can go see people for coffee or lunch without feeling guilty about not getting work done.

I understand that you're struggling to find alternative solutions to manage your feelings of isolation while working from home. Have you considered reaching out to a friend or family member to grab a quick walk or do a phone call during your breaks, just to get some fresh air or social interaction?

That's not a bad idea... but I'm not sure my friends would be up for it, and my wife is always saying I spend too much time on the phone. Plus, I feel like it would be a hassle to coordinate, you know? I'd rather just... wait and see if this other thing I'm trying works out first.

It sounds like you're hesitant to reach out to friends or family due to concerns about coordination and your wife's concerns about your phone use. Are you open to exploring ways to communicate with your loved ones about your needs and boundaries?

I mean, I guess I should talk to my wife about it... she's pregnant, so she's stressed out enough, I don't want to add to that. And as for friends, it's just easier to do things on my own, you know? I don't want to bother anyone with my problems.

It sounds like you're worried about adding to your wife's stress while she's pregnant, and also feel hesitant to reach out to friends because you don't want to bother them. Can you think of a way to frame your request for support as a way to help your wife, rather than adding to her stress?

I suppose I could tell her that I want to spend more time with her and get some support while I work on quitting smoking... it's not just about the smoking, it's about us, you know? But it's hard to say that out loud.

Based on your recent conversation, you've expressed a desire to spend more time with your wife and get her support while working on quitting smoking. Would you be open to practicing a simple, gentle conversation starter with me, like "Hey, I've been thinking about how I can stay motivated and supported while quitting smoking, can we talk about how we can work together on this?"
I think I could say that... but it would be weird to have you help me practice it. Can't we just... figure it out on our own, without pretending to have this big conversation?

You're hesitant to practice a conversation starter with me, but you mentioned wanting to spend more time with your wife and get her support. Do you think having a conversation starter like this could actually help you initiate a conversation with your wife about your needs and boundaries?
I guess it couldn't hurt to try. But what if it sounds silly or something? What if she just tells me to just do it on my own?

You're worried about sounding silly or being told to just do it on your own, which makes it harder to take the first step. What if, instead of focusing on how it might come across, you could focus on the potential benefits of having this conversation with your wife, like getting her support and feeling more connected to her?

I don't know... it's hard to think about what could be good about it when all I can think about is what could go wrong. But I guess I do want to quit smoking and I do want to be better for her, and her support would really mean a lot.

I understand that you're struggling to see the potential benefits of having a conversation with your wife about your needs and boundaries. What if we try to start with a small, low-stakes conversation, like asking her if she'd be willing to help you brainstorm ways to stay motivated and focused while quitting smoking?
That might be okay... I mean, it's not like I'm asking her to quit smoking with me or anything, just for her help with staying on track. Yeah, that sounds doable, I think.
Now that you've decided a small conversation with your wife might help, what specific words or phrases do you think you could use to ask her for her help with staying on track while quitting smoking?

I think I could just say something like... "Hey, I'm trying to quit smoking and I was wondering if you could help me brainstorm some ways to stay motivated and focused. You know, I'd really appreciate your support on this."

Given that we're now planning a specific conversation with your wife, are you comfortable with the idea of having this conversation in a casual setting, like over dinner or while watching TV together?
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.
You've made some progress in reducing your bidis intake, that's a great start. What do you think triggers your urge to smoke when you're only having 2 or 3 bidis?

The morning bidi is still a big problem for me. I always wake up and feel like I need one. I know I shouldn't but...  Sometimes I think it's just a habit now, not really the smoking itself. Does that make sense?

You're saying the morning bidi is a deeply ingrained habit for you, almost like an automatic ritual. That's really insightful - what if we tried to break that morning ritual by doing something new instead of having a bidi?

That's a good idea, but what would I do instead? Just sitting around without that bidi feels awkward. Maybe... maybe a cup of tea or something? I've been meaning to cut back on tea too, it's not good for my breathing.
You're considering replacing your morning bidi with a cup of tea, which is a good start. What if you made your new morning routine just about the tea itself, rather than combining it with a bidi?

You mean, just focus on the tea? Okay, I can try that. But what if it doesn't work? I've been doing this ritual for so long, it's hard to imagine a day without it. Can you tell me what else I can do to manage my stress when I wake up feeling like I need a bidi?

You're concerned that replacing your morning bidi with tea might not work and are looking for a backup plan to manage stress. What if we made a list of other things that help you feel calm and focused in the morning, like exercise, meditation, or deep breathing, and you tried one of those when you feel the urge to smoke?

That sounds like a good idea. I do try to meditate a bit every morning, but I usually get up, have a bidi, and then sit down for meditation. I'll have to try to switch that around. Do you think this will really work, though? I've tried to quit before and failed.

You're willing to try switching up your morning routine, starting with meditating before having a cup of tea. What if you set a small goal to meditate for just 5 minutes, without any attachments or expectations, and see how that feels?
5 minutes sounds manageable, I guess. But what if my mind just wanders and I don't feel calm? I've tried meditation before, it's harder than it looks.

Since we're in the Planning phase, I need to focus on suggesting a technique to help you manage your stress and quit bidis. You're worried that your mind might wander during meditation, which makes you feel uncertain about trying this approach. 

Would you be willing to try a gentle, guided meditation session with me, so I can help you through the process and make it feel more manageable?

That makes sense, you want to be able to stop if you feel overwhelmed. Are you comfortable with the idea of me guiding you through a meditation session, but with a clear "red light" signal, like saying "stop" or raising your hand, if you need to pause or end it?
That sounds reasonable, yes. A red light signal would make me feel more in control, I think. Okay, let's try this. But if I start to feel uncomfortable, it's on.

I appreciate that. I really do want to quit smoking, it's just hard to break this one last habit. Okay, go ahead and guide me through this meditation thing. What do I do?

It sounds like you're ready to get started on the meditation, but you're feeling a bit impatient. Do you think that's related to your morning craving or something else?
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.

You're feeling guilty about the financial burden your smoking is placing on your family, and that's causing you a lot of stress. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you see the money you spend on cigarettes each month?

It's just that 4000 rupees, for my kids' school fees, it's a lot. I could be saving that for their future. My wife would be so happy if I could cut back on spending like this. It's not that I like smoking, it's just a habit.

You're saying it's the thought of not being able to provide for your kids' education that's really affecting you. What are some situations or moments when you usually think about your family and feel this guilt?

When my kids ask me for money for something, and I have to tell them I spent it all on cigarettes. Or when we're eating dinner, and I grab a cigarette right after. I can see the disappointment in my wife's eyes. These moments, they stick with me.

You've identified two specific moments when you feel guilty and regretful about your smoking - when your kids ask for money and when you smoke right after meals. It sounds like those moments are quite vivid and painful for you. Would you be open to trying a technique called "urge surfing" to help you navigate those situations more mindfully?
I don't know if I understand what that means, "urge surfing". Sounds like some sort of new-age fad. Can you explain it to me in simple terms? What's the point of it?

Let me explain it in simple terms: "urge surfing" is a technique where you acknowledge and ride out the physical sensations of craving, rather than trying to fight them. 

Would you like to try feeling the urge, rather than acting on it, and see how that plays out for you?
I don't know...I just feel this craving, and I want to smoke. I want to make it go away. I don't see how sitting with it will make a difference. How's this supposed to save me money?

You feel overwhelmed by the craving and want to act on it. Can you think of a time when you've been able to put off smoking for a little while, even if it was just 10 or 15 minutes, and what might have helped you do that?

I think I managed to cut back to 10 a day once, for a couple of weeks. I wasn't really trying, I just got busy with work. But I remember I used to drive my route and take a different way, so I'd get home a little later. I could put off smoking for a bit, till I got home.

You've found a situation in the past where you were able to delay smoking for a bit, even if it was unintentionally, by taking a longer route home. It sounds like you have some ability to put off acting on the craving, even if it's just for a short time. What do you think might happen if you intentionally took a few minutes to focus on your surroundings and breathe between the time you feel the craving and the time you would normally smoke?

I guess it's worth a shot. Taking a couple of minutes to breathe and look around, it's not like it's going to hurt anything, right? But what's the plan after that? If I'm delaying smoking, I'm still craving it, so...
Let's take it one step at a time. Can you think of any situations where delaying smoking for those couple of minutes actually ended up helping you manage your craving in the long run?

There was this one time when I took the longer route home and I had a bit of a break. I was so focused on traffic that I almost forgot about smoking. By the time I got home, the craving had passed. It was a nice surprise.

You've had a positive experience where focusing on something else, like traffic, helped you forget about smoking. Would you be willing to try using a similar distraction, like paying attention to your surroundings, while you're in situations where you normally smoke?

I think it's worth a try. I could listen to the radio or podcasts while I have my break. Something to keep my mind occupied. That way, when I get to the point where I normally smoke, I might not even notice I'm craving it.

You're already thinking of ways to keep your mind occupied, like listening to the radio or podcasts. How might you make this a regular habit, something you can turn to whenever you feel the urge to smoke?

I could make a playlist or something. Put it on my phone and just press play when I need to. And maybe take a few minutes to stretch or move around, get the blood flowing. That might help distract me too.

You're already thinking creatively about how to use music and movement to distract yourself from cravings. What if you also set small rewards for yourself after you've successfully navigated a challenging situation without smoking?

That's actually a good idea. If I can resist smoking when I'm stressed, like during traffic, maybe I can treat myself to a good cup of coffee or a nice snack. Something small like that would be nice. And maybe it would motivate me to keep going.
Now that you've had an idea about setting small rewards for yourself, how would you make sure to actually follow through on that reward, so it really feels like a motivator?

I could write it down, like a schedule. Plan out my rewards and when I'll get them. That way, I won't forget. And maybe I can even tie it to specific milestones, like a week without smoking 10 cigarettes, or a month without spending 4000 rupees.
Now that you've thought of writing down your rewards, how will you make sure to review that schedule regularly, so you stay on track and don't forget about your rewards?
I only smoke at parties or when I'm drinking with friends, maybe 3-4 times a week. It's not like I'm a real smoker. But I've been having asthma attacks more often.

You're right, you don't smoke regularly, but those instances are causing you a lot of concern. Have you ever thought of having a "buddy system" in place for situations where you know you'll be around parties or drinks with friends, like having a trusted friend there to distract you and keep you accountable?
That's a good idea, but it's hard to predict when those situations will come up and how I'll feel. Plus, I'm not really sure if I can just stop when I want to.

You're right that it's hard to predict those situations, but can you think of anything you could do in the moment to prepare yourself for those situations, like having a plan or a mantra to help you stay on track?
I guess having a plan would be better than nothing. But it's just that sometimes I feel like I'm missing out if I don't smoke, like all my friends are doing it and having fun.

You're worried that not smoking will make you feel left out from the fun. What does it mean to you when you're having fun without smoking - is there something else that brings you joy in those social situations?
I mean, I like dancing and trying new drinks, but I guess it's not the same as smoking. When I'm with my friends, it feels like smoking is like a part of the experience, you know?

You're saying that smoking feels like a part of the experience when you're with your friends. Can you think of a way to still be a part of that experience without smoking, like maybe finding a new ritual to replace it?
Like, I could offer to be the DJ or something, or just focus on dancing and talking to people. But it's hard to break the habit, I mean, it's just something I've always done with my friends.

You're thinking of alternative ways to engage with your friends, like being the DJ or focusing on dancing and talking. How do you feel about trying to shift your focus away from the cigarette and onto one of those activities when you're in a social situation?
It sounds doable, but I'm not sure how I'll feel in the moment. It's one thing to think about it now, but when I'm actually at the party and everyone's smoking...I don't know.

You're worried about how you'll feel in the moment when you're at the party and everyone's smoking. What if you were to carry a small, non-smelling item with you, like a stress ball or a fun keychain, to serve as a reminder of your new choice and help shift your focus away from cigarettes?
That's actually kind of a good idea. I might feel silly carrying a stress ball around, but if it helps me stay on track, I'm willing to try anything.

You're open to trying a stress ball as a reminder to stay on track, which is a great step. How do you plan to handle the moment when your friends ask you why you're not smoking, and how might you respond to them?

That's the part that makes me nervous. I don't want to make a big deal out of it, but at the same time, I don't want to feel like I'm missing out. Maybe I could just say something casual, like "I'm trying to cut back" or "I'm focusing on my health right now".

You're thinking about responding to your friends in a casual way, which is a great approach. How would you feel about using a simple phrase, like "not for me," when your friends ask you why you're not smoking, to help deflect the question and stay on track?
That sounds perfect. "Not for me" is simple and it doesn't draw attention to it, which is what I'm worried about. I think I could use that.
I'm glad you feel more confident about navigating these conversations, and I wish you continued success in your decision.
Now that you feel more confident about handling those conversations, can you imagine yourself in a situation where you're at a party and someone offers you a cigarette?
